source.title: Workplace 401(k): the only loophole your employer pays you to use
source.cite: IRC Section 401(k); IRS Publication 525; IRS Topic No. 424

ad_headlines: Legal. Pre-tax. And your employer pays extra when you use it.
The 401(k) match is the rare loophole someone else funds for you.
Skipping the match is leaving a percentage of salary on the table.
variants.twitter:
An employer 401(k) match is the closest thing the tax code has to free money:
• Your contribution comes off taxable wages
• Your employer adds compensation that vanishes if you don't enroll

Source: IRC §401(k), IRS Topic 424. Educational, not advice.
variants.linkedin:
The workplace 401(k) is the only line in the tax code where your employer pays you to use the loophole.

Two legal mechanics, both sourced to IRC §401(k) and IRS Topic No. 424:

1) Pre-tax contributions come straight off your taxable wages for the year. The IRS doesn't see the money until you withdraw it later.

2) Many employers match a portion of what you contribute. That match is compensation — additional pay you've already earned that simply disappears if you don't enroll.

A few details worth knowing:

• Many plans also offer a Roth 401(k) — same contribution mechanic, but after-tax dollars in, tax-free qualified withdrawals out.
• Workers 50+ can make additional catch-up contributions on top of the regular limit.
• Contribution limits and catch-up amounts change every year — check current IRS guidance.

The minimum-bar move most people aren't doing: contribute at least enough to capture the full employer match. The math beats almost every other use of the same dollar.

Educational only. Not tax advice — confirm current-year limits and your plan's specific match formula.
variants.reddit:
**The 401(k) employer match is the rare tax break someone else funds for you**

Two mechanics, both straight out of IRC §401(k):

1. Pre-tax contributions reduce your taxable wages for the year (Roth 401(k) variant is after-tax with tax-free qualified withdrawals later).
2. Many employers match a portion of what you contribute. That match is additional compensation that disappears if you don't enroll.

The minimum-effort move: contribute at least enough to capture the full match. Everything past that is a different conversation (vesting schedule, Roth vs. pre-tax, fees in your plan menu).

50+ get catch-up contributions on top of the standard limit. Limits move yearly.

Sources: IRC §401(k), IRS Pub 525, IRS Topic No. 424. Educational — not advice.

brand_voice_principles: Plain-spoken, confident, demystifying. Translates tax-code complexity.
A little irreverent about how needlessly complicated the system is — on the reader's side.
Specific and sourced. Every claim points to a real rule.
No: 'one weird trick', 'IRS hates this', guaranteed savings, sleaze.
